docs.title: Admont-AI Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the official documentation for Admont-AI — a document engine that integrates your content with search engines and large language models.

docs.title: What is Admont-AI?
docs.text:
Admont-AI sits at the center of your document workflow, connecting four key systems:

  • Storage Backends — Git repositories (local, remote, or S3-compatible object storage) for versioning and storing documents, images, diagrams, and other files.
  • Search Engines — Pluggable search engines for hybrid search across all your content.
  • LLMs — Cloud-hosted and self-hosted language models for generating, summarizing, and enriching documents.
  • Authentication Providers — Built-in authentication as well as external identity providers via OAuth 2.0 with PKCE.

Security is at the center of Admont-AI. Authentication follows the latest OAuth 2.0 standard with PKCE, and security is enforced at every level of the system — the Web UI, the MCP server, and down to individual files and folders.

You write documents in Markdown, and Admont-AI handles versioning, search indexing, AI-assisted content generation, collaboration, and access control behind the scenes. Every edit is tracked, every change can be reverted, and multiple people can work on the same knowledge base simultaneously.

Beyond plain markdown, Admont-AI supports visual diagram editing (Mermaid, Draw.io), image annotation, LaTeX math formulas, video embedding, and AI-assisted content generation — all from a single browser-based interface.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Rich Markdown Editor — WYSIWYG editing with live preview, tables, code blocks, math formulas, and embedded media
  • Git-Backed Storage — Every document is version-controlled with full commit history, diffs, and restore capability
  • Draft Workflow — Edit safely with auto-saving drafts that are published explicitly via three-way merge
  • Hybrid Search — Find content using full-text search, semantic (AI-powered) search, or a combination of both
  • Multi-Format Support — Edit Markdown, Mermaid diagrams, Draw.io diagrams, LaTeX, images, and plain text files
  • AI Assistant — Ask questions, generate new content, or polish existing text using integrated AI models
  • File and Folder Permissions — Granular access control with per-user, per-group, and path-level permissions
  • MCP Integration — Connect AI assistants like Claude directly to your wiki via the Model Context Protocol
  • Drag-and-Drop Organization — Move files and folders visually in the sidebar

docs.title: Permissions
docs.text: Admont-AI provides granular, file-level access control with hierarchical inheritance. Permissions are stored in a .file-permissions.yaml file at the root of each repository.

docs.title: Permission Levels
docs.text:
Level Value Capabilities none 0 No access viewer 1 View files and folders contributor 2 View + create and edit files, save drafts content_manager 3 Contributor + move and delete files and folders manager 4 Full control + manage permissions

Higher levels include all capabilities of lower levels.

docs.title: How Permissions Are Resolved
docs.text:
When checking a user's access to a path, the resolver evaluates in this order:

  1. Owner — If the user is the owner of the path entry, they receive full (manager) access.
  2. Users — If the user's email appears in the users map, that level is returned.
  3. Groups — If the user belongs to any listed group, the highest group permission is returned.
  4. Default — The default level for that entry is returned.

Path walking: the resolver checks for an exact path match first, then walks up parent folders (nearest ancestor first), then falls back to the root entry.

docs.title: Permissions File Structure
docs.text:
version: 1

root:
  owner: "admin@example.com"
  default: "viewer"
  users:
    editor@example.com: "contributor"
  groups:
    engineering: "manager"

groups:
  engineering:
    - alice@example.com
    - bob@example.com

paths:
  "private/":
    owner: "alice@example.com"
    default: "none"
    users:
      bob@example.com: "viewer"
    groups:
      engineering: "content_manager"

  "private/secret.md":
    default: "none"
    users:
      alice@example.com: "manager"

docs.title: File or Folder Permissions
docs.text:
1. Right-click a file or folder in the sidebar, or use the editor menu.
  2. Select Permissions.
  3. The dialog shows the current effective permission and its source (explicit or inherited).
  4. Set the default level, owner, and per-user/per-group overrides.
  5. Save changes.

docs.title: Initializing Permissions
docs.text:
For a repository without a .file-permissions.yaml file:

  • Authenticated users have full access by default.
  • Anonymous users have read-only access on public repositories.
  • An admin can initialize the permissions file via POST /repos/:slug/permissions/init to begin enforcing granular access.

docs.title: Repository-Level Permission Groups
docs.text:
Each repository can define its own permission groups (separate from the system-wide user groups used for roles):

  1. Open the permissions dialog for any file in the repo.
  2. Add groups with specific users.
  3. Reference those groups in file/folder permission entries.

docs.title: Key Behaviors
docs.text:
• Inheritance — Child paths inherit permissions from the nearest parent unless explicitly overridden.
  • Owner privilege — Owners always receive full access to their path entry.
  • Group resolution — A user in multiple groups receives the highest permission from any matching group.
  • Folder convention — Folder paths use a trailing slash (e.g., docs/).
  • 404 for denied — Permission denials return 404 (not 403) to prevent path enumeration.
  • Auto-update on move/rename — Permission entries are automatically updated when files or folders are moved or renamed.
  • Cascade delete — Deleting a folder removes all permission entries under that path.

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text: Method Path Description POST /repos/:slug/permissions/init Initialize permissions file GET /repos/:slug/permissions/*path Get permissions for a path PUT /repos/:slug/permissions/*path Set permissions for a path DELETE /repos/:slug/permissions/*path Remove explicit permissions (revert to inherited) POST /repos/:slug/groups/:name Create permission group PUT /repos/:slug/groups/:name Update permission group members DELETE /repos/:slug/groups/:name Delete permission group

docs.title: Repository Management
docs.text: Repositories are the containers for all document content. Each repository maps to a Git backend (or S3 storage) and can be independently configured with search, access controls, and visibility settings.

docs.title: Backend Types
docs.text: Type Description remote_git Standard Git repository hosted remotely; changes are pushed on save local_git Git repo on server disk only; no remote (can be promoted to remote_git later) s3_git Local Git repo synced with S3; S3 is the source of truth s3_store Pure S3 object storage without Git; optional version history via S3 versioning

docs.title: Adding a Repository
docs.text:
1. Open the Admin panel.
  2. Go to the Repositories tab.
  3. Click Add Repository.
  4. Select a backend type.
  5. Fill in the configuration fields (see below).
  6. Click Create.

The server starts cloning/initializing the repository in the background. The repository becomes available once initialization completes.

docs.title: Configuration Fields
docs.text: Field Description name Display name shown in the UI slug URL-safe identifier (auto-generated from name if not specified) repo_url Git clone URL (for remote_git) branch Branch to track (default: main) doc_path Subdirectory containing documents (optional; defaults to root) authenticated Whether the remote requires credentials username / auth_token Credentials for authenticated repos public_access If true, anonymous users can read content read_only If true, all write operations are blocked lfs_enabled Enable Git Large File Storage support search_provider Search engine to use for this repo

docs.title: S3 Backend Fields
docs.text:
For s3_git and s3_store backends:

Field Description s3_bucket S3 bucket name s3_prefix Path prefix within the bucket s3_region AWS region s3_endpoint Custom endpoint (for S3-compatible services like MinIO) s3_access_key / s3_secret_key Credentials (optional if using IAM roles)

docs.title: Updating Settings
docs.text:
Click a repository in the admin panel to expand its details. Click Edit to modify settings. Changes that affect the search provider trigger automatic reindexing.

A local_git repository can be promoted to remote_git by setting a repo_url.

docs.title: Re-clone
docs.text: Click Reclone to delete the local copy and re-initialize the repository from the remote. This runs in the background. Use this when the local state is out of sync or corrupted.

docs.title: Reindex
docs.text: Click Reindex to rebuild the search index for a repository. This re-chunks all files, regenerates embeddings, and updates the search database. Reindexing runs in the background.

docs.title: Deleting a Repository
docs.text: Click Delete to remove a repository. This deletes the database entry, removes the local file copy, and unregisters all backends. The remote Git repository is not affected.

docs.title: Repository Status
docs.text:
The admin panel shows badges for each repository:

  • Public / Private — visibility setting
  • Read-Only — write protection enabled
  • Authenticated — remote requires credentials
  • LFS — Git LFS enabled
  • Search — search provider configured

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text:
Method Path Description GET /admin/repos List all repositories POST /admin/repos Add a new repository PUT /admin/repos/:slug/settings Update repository settings DELETE /admin/repos/:slug Remove a repository POST /admin/repos/:slug/reclone Re-clone from remote POST /admin/repos/:slug/reindex Rebuild search index

All repository management endpoints require the repo_admin role.

docs.title: User Management
docs.text: Admont-AI supports two types of user accounts: internal users (managed by the platform) and external users (linked to OAuth providers). Both types can be assigned roles and managed through the admin panel.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Role Capabilities super_admin All roles; bypasses all permission checks system_admin Manage auth providers, LLM providers, search providers user_admin Manage internal/external users and groups repo_admin Manage repositories (add, configure, re-clone, reindex)

Super admins automatically have all roles. Roles can be assigned to individual users or to groups (all group members inherit the group's roles).

docs.title: Internal Users
docs.text: Internal users authenticate with email and password, managed directly by Admont-AI via Ory Hydra.

docs.title: Creating an Internal User
docs.text:
1. Open the Admin panel from the header.
  2. Go to the Internal Users tab.
  3. Click Add User.
  4. Enter email, first name, last name, and a password.
  5. Select roles and optionally enable super admin.

Internal users support:

  • Password management — Admins can force password expiry, requiring the user to set a new password on next login.
  • Two-factor authentication (TOTP) — Users can enable TOTP-based 2FA. Admins can reset a user's TOTP configuration if they lose access to their authenticator.
  • Account suspension — Suspended users cannot log in.

docs.title: Editing and Deleting
docs.text: Click a user in the list to edit their details, roles, or suspension status. Use the delete button to remove a user permanently.

docs.title: External Users
docs.text: External users authenticate via a configured OAuth provider (Google, GitHub, Okta, Azure AD, etc.). Their accounts are created in Admont-AI and linked to the provider.

docs.title: Creating an External User
docs.text:
1. Go to the External Users tab in the admin panel.
  2. Click Add User.
  3. Select the OAuth provider from the dropdown.
  4. Enter the user's email, first name, and last name.
  5. Assign roles.

External users inherit their identity from the OAuth provider. They cannot change their password or enable TOTP through Admont-AI.

docs.title: Groups
docs.text: Groups let you manage roles and permissions for multiple users at once.

docs.title: Creating a Group
docs.text:
1. Go to the Groups tab in the admin panel.
  2. Click Add Group.
  3. Enter a name and optional description.
  4. Add members by searching for users (both internal and external).
  5. Assign roles to the group.

All members of a group inherit the group's roles. A user who belongs to multiple groups receives the union of all group roles.

docs.title: Group Uses
docs.text:
Groups serve two purposes:

  1. Role assignment — Admin roles (system_admin, user_admin, repo_admin) assigned to the group apply to all members.
  2. File permissions — Groups can be referenced in .file-permissions.yaml to grant file-level access to all members.

docs.title: API Endpoints
docs.text:
Method Path Description GET /admin/users List all users (internal + external) GET /admin/users/internal List internal users POST /admin/users/internal Create internal user PUT /admin/users/internal/:email Update internal user DELETE /admin/users/internal/:email Delete internal user DELETE /admin/users/internal/:email/totp Reset TOTP GET /admin/users/external List external users POST /admin/users/external Create external user PUT /admin/users/external/:provider/:email Update external user DELETE /admin/users/external/:provider/:email Delete external user GET /admin/groups List groups POST /admin/groups Create group PUT /admin/groups/:name Update group DELETE /admin/groups/:name Delete group

All user management endpoints require the user_admin role.

docs.title: Backend
docs.text: The backend is written in Go 1.25 and uses the Gin HTTP framework together with Fuego for automatic OpenAPI 3.1 documentation generation. The server manages Git repositories on disk, provides a full REST API for the frontend, runs an MCP server for AI assistant integration, and operates a hybrid search engine backed by PostgreSQL.

docs.title: Package Structure
docs.text: Package Purpose internal/auth Google OAuth 2.0 flow and JWT token management internal/git Git clone, pull, file read/write, diff, history, LFS support internal/draft Draft storage, metadata tracking, three-way merge publishing internal/search Hybrid search engine (chunker, embedder, indexer, store) internal/mcp MCP server with SSE transport and OAuth authentication internal/ai Claude AI integration via the Anthropic SDK internal/permissions File-level access control with hierarchical resolution internal/middleware JWT authentication and admin authorization middleware internal/request_handler HTTP request handlers for all REST endpoints config YAML-based configuration management via Viper

docs.title: Git Integration
docs.text:
All wiki content lives in standard Git repositories cloned to the server's local filesystem. On startup, each configured repository is initialized asynchronously — cloned if not yet present, or pulled if it already exists. The Git client supports:

  • Cloning with progress tracking and optional LFS pulls
  • Reading and writing files with automatic commit creation
  • Retrieving file history (commit log per file)
  • Generating diffs between any commit and HEAD
  • Fetching file content at a specific commit
  • URL-embedded credentials for authenticated repositories

Drafts are stored in .drafts/ subdirectories alongside the documents they belong to. The .drafts/ path is automatically added to .gitignore so draft content never enters the Git history until explicitly published.

docs.title: Authentication and Authorization
docs.text:
Authentication uses Google OAuth 2.0. The server redirects users to Google's consent screen, receives the authorization code on callback, exchanges it for user information, and issues a signed JWT (HS256, 24-hour expiry). The JWT carries the user's email and display name as claims.

Two middleware functions protect routes: - JWTAuth — requires a valid JWT; extracts identity into the request context. - JWTAuthOptional — allows anonymous access but populates identity if a token is present.

Authorization operates at two levels:

  1. Role-based — Users can hold super_admin, admin, or repo_admin roles configured in the user config file. Super admins bypass all permission checks.
  2. File-level — A .file-permissions.yaml file in each repository defines granular access per path. Permissions are none, read, update, or delete and resolve hierarchically: exact path match → nearest parent folder → repository default.

docs.title: Draft System
docs.text:
The draft manager provides a non-destructive editing workflow. When a user saves changes, they are written as a draft file rather than committed directly. Draft metadata records the original file path, user identity, and the base commit hash at the time the draft was created.

Publishing a draft performs a three-way merge: the base commit, the current HEAD, and the draft content are compared. If the file has changed on HEAD since the draft was created, conflict markers are inserted and returned for manual resolution. On successful merge, the content is committed and the draft file is deleted.

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text: The frontend is a React 19 single-page application built with TypeScript, Vite, and Tailwind CSS. It uses shadcn/ui (built on Radix UI primitives) for base components and MDXEditor (Lexical-based) for WYSIWYG markdown editing.

docs.title: State Management
docs.text:
The app uses React Context and custom hooks rather than an external state management library:

  • AuthContext — Global authentication state: user identity, permissions, JWT lifecycle, auto-logout before token expiry.
  • AiLogContext — AI operation history and model selection.
  • Custom hooks — useRepos, useDocuments, useDocumentContent, useDocumentSave, useDocumentMutations, useFolders, useFileHistory, useFilePermission encapsulate API calls and loading/error states.

docs.title: API Communication
docs.text:
All API calls go through a centralized authFetch() wrapper that injects the JWT Bearer token from localStorage, handles 401 responses by dispatching an auth:unauthorized event (triggering logout), and surfaces errors as toast notifications.

In development, Vite proxies API paths (/repos, /auth, /me, /admin, /ai) to the backend at localhost:8080.

docs.title: Routing
docs.text: The app uses URL-based SPA routing without a router library. The URL pattern /{repo}/{filePath} is parsed on load and updated via window.history.replaceState() on navigation. Deep linking is fully supported. File type is detected by extension and routes to the appropriate editor component.

docs.title: Multi-Format Editing
docs.text: File Type Extension Editor Component Markdown .md MDXEditor with custom math (KaTeX) and video plugins Mermaid diagrams .mmd Visual editor (React Flow) + text editor with live preview Draw.io diagrams .drawio Embedded Draw.io editor Images .png, .jpg, .svg, etc. TUI Image Editor for annotation Video .mp4, .webm, etc. HTML5 video player LaTeX .tex LaTeX editor with math rendering Plain text other Generic text editor

docs.title: Key UI Features
docs.text:
• Sidebar file tree with drag-and-drop for moving files and folders
  • Hybrid search bar with configurable modes (fulltext, semantic, hybrid) and 300ms debounce
  • Draft workflow with auto-save, publish, and discard actions
  • File history panel showing commit log with diff view and restore capability
  • AI assistant supporting ask, generate, and polish modes with model selection and token usage tracking
  • Permission management dialogs for file and folder access control

docs.title: MCP Server
docs.text: The backend includes an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that allows AI assistants like Claude to interact with wiki repositories programmatically. It uses SSE (Server-Sent Events) as the transport layer and implements its own OAuth 2.0 authorization flow on top of Google authentication.

docs.title: Transport and Connection
docs.text: The MCP server exposes two endpoints: - GET /mcp/sse — Establishes a persistent SSE connection for receiving server-to-client messages. - POST /mcp/message — Receives client-to-server MCP protocol messages.

docs.title: OAuth Authentication Flow
docs.text:
MCP clients authenticate through a dedicated OAuth flow that wraps Google Sign-In:

  1. The client discovers the auth server via /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource and /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server.
  2. The client registers dynamically via POST /mcp/register (RFC 7591) and receives a client_id.
  3. The client redirects the user to GET /mcp/authorize with a PKCE challenge.
  4. The server proxies to Google OAuth; the user authenticates in their browser.
  5. On callback, the server issues an authorization code.
  6. The client exchanges the code at POST /mcp/token for a JWT.
  7. The client reconnects to SSE with the JWT in the Authorization header.

docs.title: Exposed Tools
docs.text:
The MCP server exposes 22 tools that mirror the REST API functionality:

  • Repository tools — list_repos, get_repo
  • File read tools — read_file, get_file_info, get_file_history, get_file_diff, get_file_at_commit
  • File write tools — create_file, update_file, delete_file, move_file, rename_file, upload_file
  • Folder tools — create_folder, rename_folder, delete_folder, move_folder
  • Draft tools — save_draft, publish_draft, delete_draft
  • Search tools — search, search_status

Each connected MCP session is tracked with user identity and connection timestamp, viewable via GET /me/mcp-clients.

docs.title: REST API Reference
docs.text: The REST API is organized around repositories, files, folders, drafts, search, and administration. Fuego generates a full OpenAPI 3.1 specification available at /swagger/openapi.json.

docs.title: Core Endpoints
docs.text: Method Path Description Repositories GET /repos List all accessible repositories GET /repos/:repo List files and folders in a repository Files GET /repos/:repo/file/*path Read file content (returns draft if one exists) POST /repos/:repo/file/*path Create a new file PUT /repos/:repo/file/*path Update file content DELETE /repos/:repo/file/*path Delete a file PATCH /repos/:repo/file/*path Move a file to a new location PUT /repos/:repo/rename/*path Rename a file POST /repos/:repo/upload/*path Upload a binary file (base64 encoded) File Info & History GET /repos/:repo/fileinfo/*path Get file metadata (author, date, size, permissions) GET /repos/:repo/filehistory/*path Get commit history for a file GET /repos/:repo/filediff/*path Get diff between a commit and HEAD GET /repos/:repo/fileatcommit/*path Get file content at a specific commit Drafts PUT /repos/:repo/draft/*path Save or update a draft POST /repos/:repo/draft/publish/*path Publish a draft (three-way merge) DELETE /repos/:repo/draft/*path Discard a draft Folders POST /repos/:repo/folder/*path Create a folder PUT /repos/:repo/folder/*path Rename a folder DELETE /repos/:repo/folder/*path Delete a folder PATCH /repos/:repo/folder/*path Move a folder Search POST /repos/search Search documents (fulltext, semantic, or hybrid) GET /repos/search/status Get search index status per repository Permissions GET/PUT/DELETE /repos/:repo/permissions/*path Manage file-level permissions AI GET /ai/models List available AI models POST /ai Execute AI operation (ask, generate, polish) Auth GET /auth/login Initiate Google OAuth flow GET /auth/callback Handle OAuth callback Admin POST /admin/repos Add a new repository PUT /admin/repos/:slug/settings Update repository settings POST /admin/repos/:slug/reindex Rebuild search index

docs.title: Middleware Stack
docs.text: All requests pass through CORS handling and gzip compression (except MCP SSE endpoints, which require uncompressed streaming). Request logging uses Logrus with structured fields.

docs.title: Search Engine
docs.text: The search engine combines full-text search and semantic (vector) search with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to deliver relevant results across all indexed repositories.

docs.title: Chunker
docs.text: The chunker parses markdown files using the Goldmark AST parser and splits content into sections based on heading hierarchy. Each chunk preserves its heading path (e.g., "Installation > Prerequisites") for context. Sections exceeding 1000 characters are split at paragraph boundaries. The result is an array of chunks, each with an index, heading path, and content.

docs.title: Embedder
docs.text: Text chunks are converted to 384-dimensional vectors using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model running via ONNX Runtime. The embedder tokenizes text with a WordPiece tokenizer, runs inference in batches of 32, applies mean pooling over non-padding tokens, and L2-normalizes the output vectors.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
The search index lives in PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension. The search_chunks table stores each chunk's content, a tsvector column for full-text search, and a vector(384) column for semantic search. Indexes include a GIN index on the tsvector and an HNSW index on the embedding vector.

A separate search_repo_state table tracks each repository's last indexed commit SHA and chunk count.

docs.title: Search Modes
docs.text: Mode Description Fulltext PostgreSQL plainto_tsquery with ts_rank_cd scoring Semantic Cosine similarity via pgvector (<=> operator) against the query embedding Hybrid (default) Runs both fulltext and semantic searches at 2x the requested limit, then merges results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion: score = Σ(1 / (60 + rank)). Returns the top-k results sorted by fused score.

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text:
• Incremental — Triggered on each file create, update, or delete. Only the affected file's chunks are reindexed.
  • Startup reindex — On server start, changed files since the last indexed commit are detected and reindexed.
  • Full reindex — Available via the admin API; rebuilds the entire index for a repository.

All indexing runs asynchronously in goroutines. Per-repository status (Ready, Rebuilding, Updating, Error) is tracked and available via the search status endpoint.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text: Admont-AI is a collaborative markdown wiki platform built on Git-backed storage. It consists of a Go backend server that exposes a REST API and an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, and a React frontend that provides a rich editing experience for markdown and other document types. All content is stored in Git repositories, giving the system built-in version history, branching, and collaboration support.

docs.title: API Integration
docs.text: Admont-AI exposes a REST API that external applications can use to read and write documents, search content, and interact with the AI assistant. The API is documented with OpenAPI 3.1.

docs.title: API Documentation
docs.text:
The full OpenAPI specification is available at:

  • JSON: GET /swagger/openapi.json
  • Interactive docs: GET /swagger/index.html

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All authenticated requests require a JWT token in the Authorization header:

Authorization: Bearer <token>

docs.title: Obtaining a Token
docs.text:
1. Initiate OAuth flow: GET /auth/login?provider=google&redirect_uri=https://your-app.com/callback
  2. The user is redirected to the OAuth provider.
  3. After authentication, the user is redirected to your redirect_uri with a token query parameter.
  4. Use this token for subsequent API calls.

docs.title: Available Auth Providers
docs.text:
GET /auth/providers

Returns the list of configured OAuth providers.

docs.title: Token Lifetime
docs.text: JWTs expire after 24 hours. When a request returns 401 Unauthorized, the token has expired and the user must re-authenticate.

docs.title: List Repositories
docs.text:
GET /repos

Returns all repositories the user can access.

docs.title: Read a File
docs.text:
GET /repos/:repo/file/*path

Returns the file content. If the user has a draft, the draft content is returned instead.

docs.title: Create a File
docs.text:
POST /repos/:repo/file/*path
Content-Type: application/json

{ "content": "# New Document\n\nContent here." }

docs.title: Update a File
docs.text:
PUT /repos/:repo/file/*path
Content-Type: application/json

{ "content": "Updated content." }

docs.title: Delete a File
docs.text: DELETE /repos/:repo/file/*path

docs.title: Search Documents
docs.text:
POST /repos/search
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "repos": [{ "name": "my-docs", "path": "" }],
  "query": "search terms",
  "mode": "hybrid",
  "top_k": 10
}

Modes: hybrid (default), fulltext, semantic.

docs.title: Ask the AI
docs.text:
POST /repos/rag
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "repos": [{ "name": "my-docs", "path": "" }],
  "query": "What is the deployment process?",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
  "top_k": 10
}

Returns an answer with sources citing which documents were used.

docs.title: File Operations Reference
docs.text: Method Path Description GET /repos/:repo/file/*path Read file POST /repos/:repo/file/*path Create file PUT /repos/:repo/file/*path Update file DELETE /repos/:repo/file/*path Delete file PATCH /repos/:repo/file/*path Move file PUT /repos/:repo/rename/*path Rename file POST /repos/:repo/upload/*path Upload binary file GET /repos/:repo/fileinfo/*path File metadata GET /repos/:repo/filehistory/*path Commit history GET /repos/:repo/filediff/*path Diff vs HEAD GET /repos/:repo/fileatcommit/*path Content at commit

docs.title: Draft Operations
docs.text: Method Path Description PUT /repos/:repo/draft/*path Save draft POST /repos/:repo/draft/publish/*path Publish (three-way merge) DELETE /repos/:repo/draft/*path Discard draft

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
• 401 Unauthorized — Token missing, invalid, or expired.
  • 404 Not Found — File doesn't exist, or you don't have permission (404 is returned instead of 403 to prevent path enumeration).
  • 400 Bad Request — Invalid request body.
  • 500 Internal Server Error — Server error (check logs).

All error responses include a JSON body with error and optionally detail fields.

git clone <ui-repo-url> admont-ai-ui

docs.title: Style Guidelines
docs.text:
• Backend: follow standard Go conventions (gofmt, meaningful package names)
  • Frontend: follow the existing ESLint configuration (npm run lint)
  • Use the existing patterns in the codebase — check nearby files for conventions before adding new code

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text: This guide covers the local development workflow for contributing to Admont-AI.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.25+ — Backend
  • Node.js 22+ — Frontend
  • PostgreSQL 17+ with pgvector extension — Database
  • Docker (optional) — For running PostgreSQL, Ory Hydra, and LanguageTool

docs.title: Quick Setup with Docker Compose
docs.text:
The easiest way to run the supporting services:

cd admont-ai-api/docker-compose
docker-compose up -d

This starts:

  • PostgreSQL (port 5433) — with pgvector extension, user admin, database admont-ai
  • Ory Hydra (ports 4444/4445) — OAuth 2.0 / OIDC server for internal authentication
  • LanguageTool (port 8081) — Grammar and spelling checker (optional)

docs.title: Running the API
docs.text:
cd admont-ai-api

# Install dependencies
go mod download

# Create .env with required secrets
echo 'JWT_SECRET=your-hex-encoded-secret' > .env
echo 'AUTH_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8080' >> .env

# Run the server
make run
# or: go run .

The API starts on http://localhost:8080. On first run it:

  1. Connects to PostgreSQL and runs migrations automatically
  2. Generates a JWT secret if not provided
  3. Initializes authentication providers
  4. Clones configured repositories in the background
  5. Starts the search indexer

docs.title: Running the Frontend
docs.text:
cd admont-ai-ui

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start dev server
npm run dev

The frontend starts on http://localhost:5174 with Vite's hot module replacement. API requests are proxied to localhost:8080 via the Vite dev server config.

docs.title: Proxied Paths
docs.text:
The Vite proxy forwards these paths to the backend:

  • /auth/* — Authentication
  • /me — User info
  • /admin — Admin API
  • /llm — LLM operations
  • /checker — Grammar checker
  • /conversations — AI conversations
  • /repos — Repository and file operations

docs.title: Database Migrations
docs.text:
Migrations are in internal/store/migrations/ and run automatically on server startup. To apply manually:

psql -h localhost -p 5433 -U admin -d admont-ai \
  -f internal/store/migrations/000001_initial_schema.up.sql

docs.title: ONNX Model Setup (Search)
docs.text:
For local semantic search with pgvector, you need the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX model:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install "optimum[onnxruntime]" onnx onnxruntime transformers torch
python3 export_model.py

curl -fsSL -o ./models/vocab.txt \
  https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/resolve/main/vocab.txt

Configure the model paths in .env:

SEARCH_ONNX_RUNTIME_PATH=/opt/homebrew/lib/libonnxruntime.dylib
SEARCH_MODEL_PATH=models/model.onnx
SEARCH_VOCAB_PATH=models/vocab.txt

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description JWT_SECRET Hex-encoded secret for signing JWTs AUTH_BASE_URL Base URL for OAuth callbacks (e.g., http://localhost:8080) LANGUAGETOOL_URL Grammar checker URL (optional) SEARCH_ONNX_RUNTIME_PATH Path to libonnxruntime shared library SEARCH_MODEL_PATH Path to ONNX model file SEARCH_VOCAB_PATH Path to tokenizer vocab file ADMONT_ENCRYPTION_KEY Hex-encoded 32-byte key for encrypting secrets in DB

docs.text:
# Backend
cd admont-ai-api
go build -o admont-ai .

# Frontend
cd admont-ai-ui
npm run build   # outputs to dist/

cd admont-ai-api
go test ./...

Tests exist for permissions resolver, chunker, embedder tokenizer, and Confluence importer.

docs.title: MCP Integration
docs.text: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) allows AI assistants like Claude to interact with your wiki programmatically. The MCP server exposes 22 tools for reading, writing, and searching documents.

docs.title: Automatic OAuth (Recommended)
docs.text:
claude mcp add --transport sse md-wiki http://localhost:8080/mcp/sse

Or scope it to your project:

claude mcp add -s project --transport sse md-wiki http://localhost:8080/mcp/sse

On first connection, Claude Code will open a browser window for OAuth authentication. After authenticating, the MCP session is established automatically.

docs.title: Manual Configuration
docs.text:
Add to ~/.claude/settings.json:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "md-wiki": {
      "type": "sse",
      "url": "http://localhost:8080/mcp/sse"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: With Pre-existing Token
docs.text:
If you already have a JWT token:

claude mcp add --transport sse md-wiki http://localhost:8080/mcp/sse \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_JWT_TOKEN"

docs.title: Authentication Flow
docs.text:
The MCP server uses its own OAuth flow with dynamic client registration (RFC 7591) and PKCE:

  1. Client connects to GET /mcp/sse without a token.
  2. Server responds with 401 and a WWW-Authenticate header pointing to discovery endpoints.
  3. Client discovers OAuth metadata via /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server.
  4. Client registers dynamically at POST /mcp/register to get a client_id.
  5. Client redirects the user to GET /mcp/authorize with a PKCE challenge.
  6. User authenticates via OAuth provider (opens in browser).
  7. Server issues an authorization code (5-minute TTL).
  8. Client exchanges the code at POST /mcp/token for a JWT.
  9. Client reconnects to SSE with the JWT in the Authorization header.

docs.title: Repository Tools
docs.text: Tool Description list_repos List all accessible repositories get_repo List all files and folders in a repository

docs.title: File Read Tools
docs.text: Tool Description read_file Read file content (returns draft if one exists) get_file_info Get file metadata (size, author, dates, permissions) get_file_history Get Git commit history for a file get_file_diff Get diff between a commit and HEAD get_file_at_commit Get file content at a specific commit

docs.title: File Write Tools
docs.text: Tool Description create_file Create a new file update_file Update an existing file delete_file Delete a file move_file Move a file to a different folder rename_file Rename a file upload_file Upload a binary file (base64-encoded)

docs.title: Folder Tools
docs.text: Tool Description create_folder Create a new folder rename_folder Rename a folder delete_folder Delete a folder and its contents move_folder Move a folder to a different location

docs.title: Draft Tools
docs.text: Tool Description save_draft Save or update a draft publish_draft Publish a draft (three-way merge with HEAD) delete_draft Discard a draft

docs.title: Search Tools
docs.text: Tool Description search Search documents (fulltext, semantic, or hybrid) search_status Get search index status for all repositories

docs.title: Permissions
docs.text: MCP tools enforce the same permission model as the REST API. The authenticated user's access level determines which repositories and files they can read or modify. Operations on files the user cannot access return errors.

docs.title: Session Tracking
docs.text:
Active MCP sessions are viewable at GET /me/mcp-clients, showing:

  • Session ID
  • User email and name
  • Connection timestamp

Sessions are automatically cleaned up when the SSE connection drops.

docs.title: Example: Asking Claude About Your Wiki
docs.text:
Once connected, you can ask Claude to interact with your wiki naturally:

  • "List all repositories" — calls list_repos
  • "Read the getting-started guide" — calls read_file
  • "Search for deployment instructions" — calls search
  • "Create a new page about CI/CD" — calls create_file
  • "Show me what changed in this file" — calls get_file_history and get_file_diff

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Admont-AI is configured via a YAML file (default: config.yaml) loaded by the Viper configuration library. The configuration covers the server, authentication, repositories, LLM providers, search engines, and CORS.

docs.title: Server
docs.text:
server:
  port: 8080
  host: "0.0.0.0"

docs.title: Database
docs.text:
database:
  url: "postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/admont?sslmode=disable"

The database URL is a standard PostgreSQL connection string. The server runs migrations automatically on startup.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
auth:
  jwt_secret: "your-secret-key"
  providers:
    - name: google
      client_id: "..."
      client_secret: "..."

Admont-AI supports multiple OAuth 2.0 providers. Each provider needs a name, client_id, and client_secret. Supported providers include: Google, GitHub, GitLab, Apple, Auth0, Azure AD, Bitbucket, Cognito, Discord, Facebook, LinkedIn, Microsoft, Okta, OpenID Connect, and Slack.

The jwt_secret is used to sign JWT tokens (HMAC-SHA256). It should be a strong random string.

docs.title: Repositories
docs.text:
repos:
  - slug: "my-docs"
    name: "My Documentation"
    url: "https://github.com/org/docs.git"
    branch: "main"
    description: "Team documentation"
    public_access: false
    search_provider: "pgvector"
Field Description slug URL-safe identifier for the repo name Display name url Git clone URL (supports HTTPS with embedded credentials, SSH) branch Branch to track (default: main) description Short description shown in the UI public_access If true, anonymous users can read content search_provider Search engine to use: pgvector, elasticsearch, or typesense

docs.title: LLM Providers
docs.text:
llm:
  providers:
    - name: anthropic
      api_key: "sk-ant-..."
      models:
        - id: "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
          name: "Claude Sonnet"
    - name: openai
      api_key: "sk-..."
      models:
        - id: "gpt-4o"
          name: "GPT-4o"
    - name: ollama
      base_url: "http://localhost:11434"
      models:
        - id: "llama3"
          name: "Llama 3"

Supported LLM providers: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek, Meta, Mistral, Ollama (self-hosted), Perplexity, and xAI. Each provider requires at minimum a name and either an api_key or base_url.

LLM configuration can be updated at runtime via the admin panel without restarting the server.

docs.title: pgvector (Default)
docs.text:
search:
  pgvector:
    enabled: true

Uses PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension for both full-text and semantic search. Embeddings are generated locally using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX model.

docs.title: Elasticsearch
docs.text:
search:
  elasticsearch:
    url: "http://localhost:9200"

docs.title: Typesense
docs.text:
search:
  typesense:
    url: "http://localhost:8108"
    api_key: "..."

docs.title: CORS
docs.text:
cors:
  allowed_origins:
    - "http://localhost:5174"
    - "https://your-domain.com"

CORS origins must be explicitly configured. The server will not start if no origins are specified.

docs.title: Users and Roles
docs.text:
users:
  super_admins:
    - "admin@example.com"
  admins:
    - "editor@example.com"
  • super_admin — Bypasses all permission checks, full access to everything.
  • admin — Access to the admin panel for managing repos, users, groups, and LLM settings.
