docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Admont-AI is a collaborative document platform that combines Git-backed storage, hybrid search, and AI-powered content tools in a single browser-based interface.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
You write and organize documents in Markdown. Admont-AI handles everything behind the scenes:

  • Version control — Every edit is a Git commit. You can view history, compare changes, and restore any previous version.
  • Search — Find content across all your repositories using full-text search, semantic (AI-powered) search, or a combination of both.
  • AI assistant — Ask questions about your documents, generate new content, or improve existing text using integrated language models.
  • Access control — Control who can read, edit, or delete files and folders with granular, path-level permissions.
  • Multi-format editing — Edit Markdown, Mermaid diagrams, Draw.io diagrams, LaTeX, images, and plain text files from a single interface.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Admont-AI consists of two components:

  • A Go backend that manages Git repositories, runs the search engine, connects to language models, and exposes a REST API and MCP server.
  • A React frontend that provides a rich editing experience with WYSIWYG markdown editing, a file tree sidebar, search, and an AI assistant panel.

All document content is stored in standard Git repositories. The backend clones configured repos to disk, indexes their content for search, and serves files to the frontend via the REST API. Users authenticate via OAuth 2.0 (Google or other providers), and the backend issues JWTs for session management.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Concept Description Repository A Git repository containing documents. Each repo has its own file tree, permissions, and search index. Draft An unpublished edit. Drafts are saved separately from the Git history and published explicitly via three-way merge. Scope When asking the AI assistant a question, you can scope it to the current page, the current repository, or all documents. Hybrid search Combines full-text keyword matching with semantic (vector) similarity for more relevant results. MCP Model Context Protocol — allows AI assistants like Claude to read and write wiki content programmatically.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation — Set up the backend and frontend
  • Configuration — Configure repositories, auth providers, and LLM integrations
  • Quick Start — Create your first document

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through getting Admont-AI running and creating your first document.

docs.title: 1. Start the Database
docs.text:
Ensure PostgreSQL is running with the pgvector extension enabled:

psql -c "CREATE DATABASE admont;"
psql -d admont -c "CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;"

Run the migrations:

psql -d admont -f internal/store/migrations/000001_create_users.up.sql
psql -d admont -f internal/store/migrations/000002_search_chunks.up.sql
psql -d admont -f internal/store/migrations/000003_ai_conversations.up.sql

docs.title: 2. Configure the Server
docs.text:
Create a minimal config.yaml:

server:
  port: 8080

database:
  url: "postgres://localhost:5432/admont?sslmode=disable"

auth:
  jwt_secret: "change-me-to-a-random-secret"
  providers:
    - name: google
      client_id: "your-google-client-id"
      client_secret: "your-google-client-secret"

cors:
  allowed_origins:
    - "http://localhost:5174"

repos:
  - slug: "my-docs"
    name: "My Documentation"
    url: "/path/to/local/git/repo"
    branch: "main"
    public_access: true

docs.title: 3. Start the Backend
docs.text:
cd admont-ai-api
go run .

The server starts on http://localhost:8080. It clones (or pulls) configured repositories on startup.

docs.title: 4. Start the Frontend
docs.text:
cd admont-ai-ui
npm install
npm run dev

Open http://localhost:5174 in your browser.

docs.title: 5. Log In
docs.text: Click the login button in the top right. You'll be redirected to your OAuth provider (e.g., Google). After authenticating, you're redirected back with a session token.

docs.title: 6. Create a Document
docs.text:
1. Select a repository in the sidebar.
  2. Click the + (new document) button in the sidebar toolbar.
  3. Enter a filename (e.g., hello-world.md).
  4. Write your content in the WYSIWYG editor.
  5. Click the Save button to create a draft, then Publish to commit it to Git.

docs.title: 7. Try the AI Assistant
docs.text:
Click the sparkles icon in the editor toolbar to open the AI assistant panel. You can:

  • Ask — Ask questions about the current page or search across all documents.
  • Generate — Describe content and have the AI write it directly into the editor.
  • Polish — Select text, then use quick tools to improve writing, fix spelling, or shorten.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Documents — Learn about the editor and supported file types
  • Configuration — Add more repos, LLM providers, and search engines
  • AI Assistant — Get the most out of AI-powered features

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Admont-AI implements a layered security model that combines OAuth 2.0 authentication (via Google), JWT-based session management, and PKCE protection. This document covers how users authenticate and how tokens are managed.

docs.title: OAuth 2.0 Authentication
docs.text: The application uses Google OAuth 2.0 with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for user authentication. PKCE protects against authorization code interception attacks even in public clients.

docs.title: Flow Steps
docs.text:
1. User clicks Login — The frontend calls GET /auth/login?redirect_uri=<frontend_url>.

  2. Backend generates state and PKCE — A random 32-byte state and a 32-byte PKCE code verifier are generated. The code challenge is computed as SHA256(verifier) (S256 method). The state, verifier, and frontend redirect URI are stored in a server-side map with a 10-minute TTL.

  3. Backend returns the Google auth URL — The response contains the full Google authorization URL with the state and code challenge parameters.

  4. Browser redirects to Google — The user is sent to Google's consent screen requesting the openid, email, and profile scopes.

  5. User authenticates with Google — After consent, Google redirects back to the backend's callback URL with an authorization code and the original state.

  6. Backend exchanges the code — The server validates the state, retrieves the stored PKCE verifier, and exchanges the authorization code with Google (including the verifier for PKCE validation). Google returns access and ID tokens.

  7. Backend extracts identity — The user's email and display name are extracted from the Google ID token.

  8. Backend issues a JWT — A server-signed JWT (HS256) is created with the user's email, name, and Google subject as claims. Expiry is set to 24 hours.

  9. Redirect to frontend — The browser is redirected to the stored redirect_uri with the JWT as a query parameter.

  10. Frontend stores the token — The JWT is extracted from the URL and stored in localStorage. All subsequent API calls include it as Authorization: Bearer <token>.

docs.title: State and PKCE Security
docs.text:
• State parameter prevents CSRF attacks — each login attempt uses a unique, random state value that must match on callback.
  • PKCE (S256) prevents authorization code interception — even if an attacker captures the code, they cannot exchange it without the original verifier.
  • TTL cleanup — Expired states are automatically purged every minute. States older than 10 minutes are rejected.

docs.title: Token Structure
docs.text:
Tokens are signed with HMAC-SHA256 using a server-side secret configured in auth.jwt_secret.

Claims:

Claim Description email User's email address (from Google) name User's display name (from Google) sub Google OAuth subject (unique user identifier) iat Issued-at timestamp exp Expiration timestamp (24 hours after issuance)

docs.title: Token Validation
docs.text:
On every authenticated request, the middleware:

  1. Extracts the token from the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  2. Verifies the signing method is HMAC (rejects other algorithms to prevent algorithm confusion attacks).
  3. Validates the signature using the server secret.
  4. Checks that the token has not expired.
  5. Extracts the claims and injects them into the request context as google_email, google_name, and google_subject.

docs.title: Frontend Token Lifecycle
docs.text:
• The token is stored in localStorage upon login.
  • The frontend decodes the JWT to extract user info (without an external library).
  • An auto-logout timer fires 1 minute before token expiry.
  • A 401 response from any API call triggers an immediate logout via the auth:unauthorized event.

docs.title: Security Best Practices
docs.text: Practice Implementation PKCE (S256) Both browser and MCP OAuth flows use SHA256 code challenges State parameter Random 32-byte state prevents CSRF on OAuth callbacks Time-limited tokens States: 10 min, MCP auth codes: 5 min, JWTs: 24 hours Algorithm pinning JWT validation rejects non-HMAC signing methods CORS allowlist Explicit origin configuration required (fatal if missing) Auto-cleanup Expired states, auth codes, and MCP sessions are garbage collected

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text: Admont-AI enforces authorization at two levels: endpoint protection via middleware tiers and per-request permission checks based on file paths, user identity, and role.

docs.title: Endpoint Protection
docs.text: All HTTP requests pass through a global middleware stack before reaching route-specific authentication layers. Permission checks then occur inside the request handler itself.

docs.title: Global Middleware Stack
docs.text:
Every request passes through these layers in order:

Middleware Purpose Recovery Catches panics and returns a 500 error instead of crashing the server Logging Logs request method, path, status, and duration via Logrus Gzip Compresses responses (disabled for /mcp/ SSE endpoints which require uncompressed streaming) CORS Validates the request origin against a configured allowlist

docs.title: CORS Configuration
docs.text:
CORS is configured with an explicit origin allowlist loaded from config.yaml. The configuration is:

  • Allowed origins — Must be explicitly configured (the server exits on startup if empty)
  • Allowed methods — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, OPTIONS
  • Allowed headers — Origin, Content-Type, Authorization
  • Credentials — Enabled (allows cookies for cross-origin requests)

docs.title: Route Authentication Tiers
docs.text: Routes are organized into four authentication tiers:

docs.title: Public (No Authentication)
docs.text:
No JWT is required. These endpoints handle health checks, OAuth flow initiation, API documentation, and MCP OAuth discovery.

  • GET /health
  • GET /auth/login, GET /auth/callback
  • GET /swagger/*
  • MCP OAuth endpoints (/mcp/register, /mcp/authorize, /mcp/callback, /mcp/token)

docs.title: JWTAuthOptional (Enriched but Not Required)
docs.text:
If a valid JWT is present, the user's identity is extracted into the request context. Anonymous requests pass through without error. This is used for read operations on public repositories where authentication enables personalized results (e.g., draft visibility).

  • GET /repos — List repositories
  • GET /repos/:repo — List files
  • GET /repos/:repo/file/* — Read file content
  • GET /repos/:repo/fileinfo/*, filehistory/*, filediff/*, fileatcommit/*
  • POST /repos/search
  • POST /ai, GET /ai/models

docs.title: JWTAuth (Required)
docs.text:
A valid JWT must be present or the request is rejected with 401 Unauthorized. Used for all write operations and user-specific endpoints.

  • All file mutations (create, update, delete, move, rename, upload)
  • Draft operations (save, publish, discard)
  • Folder operations (create, rename, delete, move)
  • Permission management
  • GET /me/* endpoints

docs.title: AdminCheck (Required + Admin Role)
docs.text:
In addition to a valid JWT, the user must hold an admin role. Non-admin users receive 403 Forbidden.

  • User management (/admin/users/*)
  • Group management (/admin/groups/*)
  • Repository configuration (/admin/repos/*)
  • Re-clone and reindex triggers

docs.title: Per-Request Permission Checks
docs.text: Beyond route-level authentication, each request handler performs fine-grained permission checks based on the target file, folder, or repository.

docs.title: Check Order
docs.text:
The checkPermission() function runs the following checks in sequence:

  1. Read-only repository — If the repository is configured as read-only, all write operations (Update, Delete) are blocked regardless of user identity.

  2. System admin bypass — Users listed as super_admin in the server configuration bypass all permission checks and receive full access.

  3. Dotfile protection — Access to files or directories starting with . (e.g., .git, .file-permissions.yaml) is always rejected. This prevents access to Git internals and permission configuration through the API.

  4. No permissions file — If the repository has no .file-permissions.yaml:

  5. Authenticated users receive full access (Delete level).

  6. Anonymous users receive read-only access (on public repos).

  7. Permission resolver — If a permissions file exists, the resolver evaluates the user's effective permission level for the requested path.

docs.title: Security Response Pattern
docs.text: Permission denials return 404 Not Found rather than 403 Forbidden. This prevents path enumeration — an unauthorized user cannot determine whether a file exists by observing different error codes.

docs.title: Required Permission Levels by Operation
docs.text: Operation Required Level Scope Read file / file info / history Read Target file Search Read Matched files Create file Update Parent folder Update file / save draft / publish draft Update Target file Rename / move file Update Both source and destination Delete file Delete Target file Create folder Update Parent folder Rename / move folder Update Both source and destination Delete folder Delete Target folder

docs.title: Document and Folder Permissions
docs.text: The permissions system provides granular, file-level access control with hierarchical inheritance. Permissions are stored in a .file-permissions.yaml file at the root of each repository.

docs.title: Permission Levels
docs.text:
Permissions use a hierarchical model where higher levels include all lower capabilities:

Level Value Capabilities None 0 No access Read 1 View file content and metadata Update 2 Read + create, edit, save drafts Delete 3 Update + delete files and folders

docs.title: Permissions File Structure
docs.text:
version: 1

root:                              # Global defaults
  owner: "admin@example.com"       # Owner always gets Delete access
  default: "read"                  # Default for unlisted users
  users:                           # Per-user overrides
    editor@example.com: "update"
    viewer@example.com: "read"
  groups:                          # Per-group overrides
    editors: "update"
    viewers: "read"

groups:                            # Group membership definitions
  editors:
    - alice@example.com
    - bob@example.com
  viewers:
    - charlie@example.com

paths:                             # Path-specific permissions
  "private/":                      # Folder (trailing slash)
    owner: "alice@example.com"
    default: "none"                # No access by default
    users:
      bob@example.com: "read"
    groups:
      editors: "update"
  "private/secret.md":            # Specific file
    default: "none"
    users:
      alice@example.com: "delete"

docs.title: Resolution Algorithm
docs.text:
When checking a user's permission for a path, the resolver evaluates in this order:

For each path level (exact match -> parent folders -> root):

  1. Owner — If the user is the owner of the path entry, they receive Delete (full) access.
  2. Users — If the user's email appears in the users map, the specified level is returned.
  3. Groups — If the user belongs to any group listed in the groups map, the highest group permission is returned.
  4. Default — The default level for that path entry is returned.

Path walking:

  • First, check for an exact match on the file path.
  • If no match, walk up parent folders (nearest ancestor first). For example, for the path docs/guides/setup.md, the resolver checks: docs/guides/ -> docs/ -> root.
  • If no folder matches, fall back to the root entry.
  • If nothing matches at all, return None (no access).

docs.title: Key Behaviors
docs.text:
• Inheritance — Child paths inherit permissions from the nearest parent unless explicitly overridden.
  • Owner privilege — Owners always receive full (Delete) access to their path entry.
  • Group resolution — A user in multiple groups receives the highest permission from any matching group.
  • Folder convention — Folder paths are stored with a trailing slash (e.g., docs/) to distinguish them from file paths.
  • Thread safety — The permission resolver uses a read-write mutex (sync.RWMutex) for safe concurrent access.
  • Atomic persistence — Permission file saves use a write-to-temp + rename pattern to prevent corruption on crash.

docs.title: Permission Management Operations
docs.text:
Permissions can be managed through the REST API:

Operation Endpoint Initialize permissions POST /repos/:repo/permissions/init Set file/folder permissions PUT /repos/:repo/permissions/*path Remove file/folder permissions DELETE /repos/:repo/permissions/*path Create group POST /repos/:repo/groups/:name Update group members PUT /repos/:repo/groups/:name Delete group DELETE /repos/:repo/groups/:name

When a file or folder is renamed or moved, its permission entry is automatically updated to reflect the new path. When a folder is deleted, all permission entries under that path are also removed.

docs.title: MCP Authentication
docs.text: The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server uses a separate OAuth flow tailored for programmatic clients like Claude Code and IDE extensions. It implements RFC 7591 Dynamic Client Registration and requires PKCE for all authorization flows.

docs.title: Flow Differences from Browser OAuth
docs.text: Aspect Browser OAuth MCP OAuth Client registration Pre-configured Dynamic (RFC 7591) Client authentication N/A None (PKCE only) Token delivery Redirect with token in URL Authorization code -> token exchange Discovery Hardcoded frontend URL .well-known metadata endpoints Auth code TTL N/A 5 minutes

docs.title: MCP OAuth Steps
docs.text:
1. Discovery — The MCP client connects to GET /mcp/sse without a token and receives a 401 with a WWW-Authenticate header pointing to /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource. From there, the client discovers the authorization server metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server.

  2. Dynamic registration — The client registers via POST /mcp/register with its redirect URIs. The server issues a random client_id with token_endpoint_auth_method: "none" (PKCE is used instead of client secrets).

  3. Authorization — The client generates a PKCE verifier and challenge, then redirects the user to GET /mcp/authorize with the challenge. The server initiates a Google OAuth flow internally.

  4. Google authentication — The user authenticates with Google in their browser. Google redirects back to the MCP callback endpoint.

  5. Code generation — The server exchanges the Google code for tokens, validates the Google ID token, generates a server JWT, creates a short-lived authorization code (5-minute TTL), and redirects the MCP client to its redirect_uri with the authorization code.

  6. Token exchange — The client sends POST /mcp/token with the authorization code and the original PKCE verifier. The server verifies SHA256(verifier) == stored_challenge, and if valid, returns the JWT as access_token.

  7. Authenticated connection — The client reconnects to GET /mcp/sse with Authorization: Bearer <JWT>. The server validates the token and establishes the SSE session.

docs.title: MCP Session Tracking
docs.text:
Each authenticated MCP connection is tracked with: - Unique session ID - User email and name - Connection timestamp - Automatic cleanup when the connection drops (via Go context cancellation)

Active sessions are viewable via GET /me/mcp-clients.

docs.title: Penetration Test Report
docs.text:
A grey-box penetration test of the Admont-AI backend (admont-ai-api), combining a known-CVE dependency scan with live dynamic testing against a running instance.

Date: 2026-06-07 Type: Grey-box (source access + running instance with forged tokens) Environment: Local stack — API on :8088, PostgreSQL :5433, Hydra :4444/4445, LanguageTool Out of scope: Infrastructure/TLS/network layer, third-party OAuth providers, physical/social engineering.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
1. Known-CVE scan — govulncheck across all reachable code paths.
  2. Live dynamic testing — the server was started and probed with crafted HTTP requests. The JWT signing secret was known to the tester, allowing forged tokens for arbitrary identities to exercise authenticated endpoints and access-control boundaries (grey-box).
  3. Each finding was re-tested after remediation.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Area Result Dependency CVEs (reachable) 35 → 0 after remediation Authentication gates ✅ All protected endpoints reject unauthenticated/forged-invalid tokens Authorization (RBAC / IDOR) ✅ Non-admin forged token denied admin; conversations scoped per-user JWT hardening ✅ alg=none and garbage tokens rejected Open redirect (OAuth + MCP) ✅ Blocked Rate limiting / brute-force 🔴 Bypassable via X-Forwarded-For spoofing → ✅ Fixed & re-verified CORS ✅ Untrusted origins not reflected Error handling ✅ No internal details leaked Path traversal ✅ Returns 404

One new exploitable vulnerability was discovered during live testing (proxy-header trust) and has been fixed. All other controls behaved correctly at runtime.

docs.title: Finding 1 — Rate-limit & brute-force bypass via spoofed X-Forwarded-For 🔴 Fixed
docs.text:
Severity: Medium–High · Status: ✅ Fixed and re-verified

The application ran with Gin's default trust-all-proxies setting. c.ClientIP() therefore honored the client-supplied X-Forwarded-For header, which is the key for both the API rate limiters and the Hydra login brute-force limiter (the control protecting passwords and TOTP).

Proof of concept (before fix):

30 requests to POST /auth/refresh, rotating X-Forwarded-For: 10.0.0.N
→ 0 requests throttled (all 401, no 429)
vs. without spoofing: 20 × 401, then 10 × 429

An attacker could rotate the header to defeat login throttling and brute-force credentials or TOTP codes.

Fix: Trusted proxies are now configurable and default to trusting none (SetTrustedProxies(nil)), so ClientIP() uses the real connection address. Deployments behind a known reverse proxy must set trusted_proxies explicitly.

Re-test (after fix): rotating X-Forwarded-For now yields 20 × 401 then 10 × 429 — throttling restored.

docs.title: Finding 2 — X-Forwarded-Proto trusted for HSTS / Secure-cookie derivation ⚪ Informational
docs.text:
Severity: Low (informational) · Status: Accepted

HSTS and the CSRF cookie's Secure flag are derived partly from the X-Forwarded-Proto header, which Gin's GetHeader reads regardless of trusted-proxy settings. An attacker can set X-Forwarded-Proto: https over plain HTTP and cause an HSTS header to be emitted.

Assessment: Not attacker-exploitable for gain — forcing HSTS-on or Secure-cooke-on does not benefit an attacker (it only upgrades to HTTPS / restricts the cookie). Recommended longer-term: derive HTTPS from c.Request.TLS and consult forwarded headers only behind a trusted proxy.

docs.title: Dependency Scan (govulncheck)
docs.text:
The initial scan reported 35 reachable vulnerabilities: a small number in golang.org/x/net@v0.50.0 (including an HTML XSS reachable through the Confluence importer and an HTTP/2 DoS) and the remainder in the Go standard library bundled with the go1.25.0 toolchain.

Remediation applied:

Action Effect golang.org/x/net → v0.55.0 (+ x/crypto, x/text, x/sys, x/sync) Cleared all third-party reachable CVEs (incl. HTML-parse XSS in Confluence import) Go toolchain pinned go 1.25.11 (via toolchain directive) Cleared all 28 standard-library CVEs

Result: Your code is affected by 0 vulnerabilities. Build and full test suite pass on go1.25.11.

Recommendation: Add govulncheck ./... to CI to catch future advisories automatically, and keep the toolchain directive current.

docs.title: Live Test Matrix
docs.text: # Test Expected Result 1 Security headers on responses Present ✅ nosniff, DENY, Referrer-Policy 2 POST /mcp/message no token 401 ✅ 401 3 POST /mcp/message valid token not 401 ✅ 400 (passes auth) 4 GET /me/details no token 401 ✅ 401 5 GET /me/details forged token 200 ✅ 200 6 GET /admin/users forged non-admin 401/403 ✅ 403 7 Garbage JWT 401 ✅ 401 8 alg=none forged JWT 401 ✅ 401 9 POST /auth/exchange bad code 400 ✅ 400 10 MCP /authorize unregistered redirect 400 ✅ 400 11 /auth/refresh rate limiting throttled ✅ 20→429 12 CORS reflection of evil origin not echoed ✅ not echoed 13 XFF-spoofed rate-limit bypass throttled 🔴→✅ fixed 14 Conversations cross-user (IDOR) scoped ✅ empty for attacker 15 Path traversal in repo file path blocked ✅ 404 16 Error message leakage generic ✅ no internals

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: The application's authentication, authorization, injection, and redirect defenses held up under live testing. The penetration test added value beyond static review by uncovering the proxy-header trust issue (Finding 1), which static analysis would not reveal, and by clearing the entire reachable-CVE backlog via dependency and toolchain upgrades. Remaining recommendations: wire govulncheck into CI, configure trusted_proxies for the production topology, and consider an external black-box test before a production launch.

docs.title: Security Review Summary
docs.text:
This document summarizes the security reviews performed on the Admont-AI backend (admont-ai-api) and the status of every finding. Three review rounds were conducted, covering source-code analysis, web/API attack surface, and account/privilege flows.

Last updated: 2026-06-07 Scope: admont-ai-api — Go backend (Gin + Fuego), PostgreSQL/pgvector, Ory Hydra internal auth, MCP server, multi-provider OAuth, at-rest secret encryption. Methodology: Manual source review augmented by parallel exploratory analysis across authentication, authorization, input handling, cryptography, deployment configuration, MCP transport, and dependencies. Every finding was verified against the code before remediation; reported severities reflect verified impact.

docs.title: Status at a Glance
docs.text:
Round Focus Findings Fixed Open / By design 1 Source-code security audit 14 14 0 2 Web & API security 10 10 0 3 MCP, account & privilege flows, dependencies 9 9 2 deferred (by decision) Total 33 33 2 deferred

Overall posture: All confirmed exploitable issues are remediated. Two behavior-changing hardening items were explicitly deferred by the maintainer (see Deferred Items). The codebase builds cleanly, passes go vet, and all unit tests pass.

Severity legend: 🔴 Critical · 🟠 High · 🟡 Medium · ⚪ Low

docs.title: Round 1 — Source-Code Security Audit
docs.text: # Sev Finding Status Location 1 🔴 Zip slip path traversal + unbounded read in Confluence import ✅ Fixed repo_requesthandler.go 2 🟠 PKCE bypass — code_verifier not required when challenge set (MCP OAuth) ✅ Fixed mcp/server.go 3 🟠 Reflected XSS in Hydra login/signup/TOTP forms (4 sites) ✅ Fixed auth/hydra_login.go 4 🟠 Git argument injection via commit hash beginning with - ✅ Fixed git/client.go 5 🟠 No server-side password validation (only HTML minlength) ✅ Fixed auth/hydra_login.go 6 🟠 Encryption key silently falls back to JWT secret ✅ Fixed main.go 7 🟡 Default DB credentials hardcoded (admin/admin) ✅ Fixed config.go, docker-compose.yaml 8 🟡 Git credentials leaked in error output ✅ Fixed git/client.go 9 🟡 Bare-email identity matching without deprecation warning ✅ Fixed auth/identity.go 10 🟡 LLM prompt injection — user instructions in system prompt ✅ Fixed llm_requesthandler.go 11 🟡 Missing security headers (nosniff, frame-options, HSTS, referrer) ✅ Fixed main.go 12 — 24h JWT with no refresh → reduced to 1h access + 7-day refresh ✅ Fixed auth/jwt.go, auth/handler.go 13 — CSRF protection added to Hydra login/signup forms ✅ Fixed auth/hydra_login.go 14 — KDF upgrade SHA-256 → HKDF-SHA256 (backward compatible) ✅ Fixed store/crypto.go

docs.title: Round 2 — Web & API Security
docs.text:
# Sev Finding Status Location 15 🟠 Open redirect + token theft in OAuth callback (redirect_uri unvalidated) ✅ Fixed auth/handler.go 16 🟠 Open redirect in MCP OAuth flow (client redirect URI unvalidated) ✅ Fixed mcp/server.go 17 🟠 Search/RAG authorization bypass (IDOR) — any authenticated user could query any non-public repo ✅ Fixed search_requesthandler.go, rag_requesthandler.go 18 🟡 CSRF cookie missing Secure flag and SameSite ✅ Fixed auth/hydra_login.go 19 🟡 Tokens in URL — JWT + refresh leaked in redirect query string ✅ Fixed (one-time exchange code) auth/handler.go 20 🟡 Goth session JSON injection via fmt.Sprintf with tokens ✅ Fixed (json.Marshal) auth/registry.go 21 🟡 MCP token endpoint returned expires_in: 86400 vs 1h actual ✅ Fixed mcp/server.go 22 ⚪ errorPage() HTML concatenation without escaping (latent XSS) ✅ Fixed auth/hydra_login.go 23 ⚪ Internal Go error details exposed to API clients (~17 handlers) ✅ Fixed request_handler/*.go 24 ⚪ No rate limiting on /auth/refresh, /repos/search, /repos/rag ✅ Fixed (token-bucket) main.go, middleware/ratelimit.go

Token exchange flow (finding 19): After OAuth, the backend now redirects with a single-use code (30-second TTL) instead of tokens. The frontend exchanges it at POST /auth/exchange for the access + refresh tokens, keeping credentials out of URLs, browser history, and access logs.

docs.title: Round 3 — MCP, Account & Privilege Flows, Dependencies
docs.text:
# Sev Finding Status Location 25 🔴 Privilege escalation — a user_admin could grant itself super_admin/roles via user & group create/update ✅ Fixed (caller must be super admin) admin_requesthandler.go 26 🟠 MCP /mcp/message endpoint had no authentication ✅ Fixed (requireAuthMessage) mcp/server.go 27 🟠 MCP canAccessRepo IDOR — non-public repos only required "is authenticated" ✅ Fixed (enforces permission resolver) mcp/server.go 28 🟡 Last super-admin lockout — could delete/demote the only super admin ✅ Fixed (guard) admin_requesthandler.go 29 🟡 First-user signup race — concurrent signups could each become super admin ✅ Fixed (mutex) auth/hydra_login.go 30 🟡 Session invalidation on password change — tokens outlived a password reset ✅ Fixed (per-identity revocation, access + refresh) auth/jwt.go, main.go, admin_requesthandler.go 31 ⚪ MCP searchStatus tool leaked all repo names/index state ✅ Fixed (filtered by access) mcp/tools.go 32 ⚪ MCP deleteDraft tool skipped canAccessRepo/checkPermission (drafts are self-scoped, so impact limited) ✅ Fixed (parity with siblings) mcp/tools.go 33 ⚪ EnsureDatabase interpolated DSN database name into DDL (operator-controlled; defense-in-depth) ✅ Fixed (identifier validation) store/store.go

Session invalidation (finding 30): JWTService maintains a per-identity cutoff; tokens issued at or before a InvalidateSessions call are rejected by both ValidateToken and ValidateRefreshToken. Triggered by self-service PUT /me/password and admin password reset. JWT iat 1-second resolution is handled explicitly. Note: the cutoff is in-memory, so a process restart clears it — acceptable because tokens expire within their lifetime (≤ 1h access, ≤ 7d refresh) regardless.

docs.title: Deferred Items (by decision)
docs.text:
These hardening changes alter user-facing behavior and were intentionally not implemented:

Item Rationale for deferral Recommendation Re-authentication for TOTP setup (POST /me/totp/setup) Currently requires only a valid JWT; a stolen token could bind attacker-controlled 2FA. Adds friction to enabling 2FA. Implement if account-takeover via stolen JWT is in the threat model; mitigated in practice by short token lifetime + session invalidation. Hydra consent screen Consent auto-accepts requested scopes with no user review. Only matters if non-first-party OAuth clients use the internal Hydra. Low priority while Hydra serves first-party clients only. Revisit before exposing dynamic client registration to third parties.

docs.title: Verified Clean
docs.text:
The following were reviewed and found to have no exploitable issues:

  • SQL injection — all queries use parameterized placeholders ($1, $2, …); the only string-built DDL (EnsureDatabase) is now identifier-validated.
  • Command injection — all exec.Command calls use argument arrays (no shell); commit refs are hex-validated.
  • Path traversal — zip extraction, draft storage, and file operations are confined; dotfile/.git paths are blocked.
  • Dependencies — current versions; golang-jwt/jwt/v5 (no algorithm-confusion exposure); no replace directives; go.sum present and locked.
  • Conversation store — all queries scope by user_email, preventing cross-user access.
  • Admin endpoints — gated by role middleware (system_admin, user_admin, repo_admin).

docs.title: Defense-in-Depth Controls Now in Place
docs.text:
• AES-256-GCM encryption at rest with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation
  • JWT (HS256) with 1h access / 7d refresh tokens and per-identity revocation
  • PKCE (S256) on all OAuth flows, including MCP
  • CSRF double-submit cookie on Hydra forms (Secure + SameSite=Lax over HTTPS)
  • Redirect-URI allow-listing on OAuth and MCP flows
  • Hierarchical, escalating per-path permission model enforced consistently across HTTP and MCP transports
  • Security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, conditional HSTS)
  • Token-bucket rate limiting on sensitive endpoints
  • Per-IP login rate limiting with TOTP 2FA support

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
After each round the following were run and passed:

go build ./...    # compiles cleanly
go vet ./...      # no vet warnings
go test ./...     # all unit tests pass

New security-focused tests were added for: zip-slip blocking, commit-ref validation, HKDF encryption round-trips, redirect-URI allow-listing, rate limiting, super-admin authorization guards, DSN identifier validation, and session invalidation (access + refresh).

docs.title: AI Assistant
docs.text: The AI assistant helps you write, understand, and explore your documentation. It is available as a side panel in the editor and supports multi-turn conversations with context from your documents.

docs.title: Opening the Assistant
docs.text: Click the sparkles icon in the editor toolbar (or the "What is this page about?" prompt) to open the AI assistant panel on the right side of the screen.

docs.title: Ask
docs.text:
Ask questions about your documents. The assistant uses RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to search relevant content and provide grounded answers with source citations.

Type a question and press Enter. The assistant searches your documents, retrieves relevant excerpts, and generates an answer. Sources are listed below each response — click a source to navigate to that document.

docs.title: Scope Selector
docs.text:
Control what context the AI uses when answering:

Scope Behavior This page Uses only the current page content (no document search) This repo Searches within the current repository All docs Searches across all accessible repositories

The scope selector is a dropdown next to the model selector at the bottom of the panel.

docs.title: Generate
docs.text: Describe content you want and the AI writes it directly into the editor at the cursor position. Useful for creating new sections, boilerplate, or structured content.

docs.title: Polish (Improve Writing)
docs.text:
Select text in the editor, then use the polish tools:

Tool Description Improve writing Rewrites the selection for clarity and flow Fix spelling & grammar Corrects errors without changing meaning Shorten Condenses the text while keeping key information Summarize Generates a summary of the selected text Explain Provides an explanation of the selected text

Polish tools replace the selected text in the editor with the improved version.

docs.title: Conversations
docs.text: The AI assistant supports multi-turn conversations. Follow-up questions retain context from earlier messages in the same conversation.

docs.title: Conversation Management
docs.text:
• New conversation — Click the + button or select "New conversation" from the dropdown.
  • Switch conversations — Click the conversation dropdown in the panel header to see recent conversations and switch between them.
  • Delete conversations — Hover over a conversation in the dropdown and click the trash icon.

Conversations are persisted server-side and available across browser sessions.

docs.title: Auto-Summarization
docs.text: When a conversation exceeds 20 messages, older messages are automatically summarized by the AI to keep token usage manageable. A "Earlier conversation summarized" divider appears where summarization occurred. Recent messages (the last 6) are always kept in full.

docs.title: Model Selection
docs.text:
Use the model dropdown at the bottom of the panel to choose which language model to use. Available models depend on which LLM providers are configured by the administrator. Your selection is saved in the browser and persists across sessions.

Supported providers: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek, Meta, Mistral, Ollama, Perplexity, xAI.

docs.title: Token Usage
docs.text: Each assistant response shows token counts: input tokens (sent to the model) and output tokens (generated by the model), displayed as ↑ and ↓ respectively.

docs.title: Documents
docs.text: Documents are the core of Admont-AI. Every file lives in a Git repository and is accessed through the browser-based editor.

docs.title: Supported File Types
docs.text:
File Type Extensions Editor Markdown .md WYSIWYG editor (MDXEditor) with live preview Mermaid diagrams .mmd Visual node editor (React Flow) + code editor with live preview Draw.io diagrams .drawio Embedded Draw.io visual editor Images .png, .jpg, .jpeg, .gif, .svg, .webp Image viewer with annotation tools Video .mp4, .webm, .mov HTML5 video player LaTeX .tex LaTeX editor with math rendering Plain text all others Generic text editor

The file type is detected by extension and the appropriate editor loads automatically.

docs.title: Creating a Document
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the target folder in the sidebar.
  2. Click the + (new document) button in the sidebar toolbar.
  3. Enter a filename including the extension (e.g., my-page.md).
  4. The editor opens in edit mode.

You can also use the AI assistant to generate a new document. Click Generate and describe the content you want.

docs.title: Editing
docs.text:
Click the pencil icon in the editor toolbar to switch to edit mode. The markdown editor provides WYSIWYG editing with:

  • Text formatting — Bold, italic, strikethrough, inline code
  • Headings — H1 through H6
  • Lists — Ordered, unordered, and task lists (checkboxes)
  • Tables — Insert and edit tables visually
  • Code blocks — Syntax-highlighted fenced code blocks
  • Blockquotes — Nested block quotes
  • Links — Inline and reference links
  • Math — LaTeX math formulas rendered via KaTeX (inline $...$ and display $$...$$)
  • Embedded media — Images, videos, and diagrams inline in the document
  • Horizontal rules — Visual section separators

docs.title: Saving and Publishing
docs.text:
Admont-AI uses a draft workflow:

  1. Save — Saves your changes as a draft. The draft is stored separately and does not affect the published version.
  2. Publish — Commits your draft to Git via a three-way merge. If someone else changed the file since you started editing, conflicts are detected and presented for resolution.
  3. Cancel — Discards your current editing session (draft is preserved if previously saved).

See Drafts for details on the draft workflow.

docs.title: Renaming and Moving
docs.text:
To rename a file, open the menu (three dots) in the editor toolbar and select Rename. Enter the new filename.

To move a file, drag it in the sidebar file tree and drop it in the target folder.

docs.title: Deleting
docs.text: To delete a file, open the menu in the editor toolbar and select Delete. You'll be asked to confirm. Deletion creates a Git commit removing the file.

docs.title: File Info
docs.text:
Select File info from the editor menu to see metadata:

  • File path and size
  • Creation and last modified dates
  • Last author and commit message
  • Commit hash
  • Your permission level

docs.title: Exporting
docs.text: Select Export as PDF from the editor menu to generate a PDF version of the current document.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: Click any file in the sidebar tree to open it. The URL updates to /{repo}/{filePath}, supporting deep linking and browser navigation (back/forward). Links between documents within the wiki are resolved automatically.

docs.title: Drafts
docs.text: Drafts provide a non-destructive editing workflow. When you save changes, they are stored as a draft file rather than committed directly to Git. This lets you work incrementally without affecting the published version.

docs.title: How Drafts Work
docs.text:
1. Edit a document by clicking the pencil icon.
  2. Save your changes. This creates a draft stored in a .drafts/ subdirectory alongside the document. The draft is invisible to other users and excluded from Git history.
  3. Continue editing at any time. Reopening a file with a saved draft loads the draft content instead of the published version.
  4. Publish when ready. This commits your changes to Git.
  5. Discard if you want to throw away your draft and revert to the published version.

docs.title: Draft Indicators
docs.text:
When a document has an unpublished draft:

  • A "Draft" badge appears next to the filename in the editor toolbar.
  • The draft saved timestamp is shown instead of the last modified date.
  • The sidebar may indicate draft status.

docs.title: Publishing
docs.text:
Publishing a draft performs a three-way merge:

  1. The base — the version of the file when you started editing (recorded as a commit hash).
  2. The current HEAD — the latest published version, which may have changed if someone else edited the file.
  3. The draft — your unpublished changes.

If the file has not changed on HEAD since you started editing, the merge is trivial and your draft is committed directly.

If the file has changed (someone else published an edit while you had a draft), the three-way merge detects conflicts. If conflicts exist, conflict markers are inserted and returned for you to resolve manually.

docs.title: Auto-Save
docs.text:
The editor auto-saves your draft periodically as you type. You can also save manually with the save button. Draft metadata tracks:

  • The original file path
  • Your user identity
  • The base commit hash (for three-way merge)
  • The timestamp of the last save

docs.title: Discarding a Draft
docs.text: Click the trash icon next to the draft badge to discard it. This permanently deletes the draft file and reverts to the published version. You'll be asked to confirm before discarding.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• Drafts are stored in .drafts/ subdirectories within the repository on disk.
  • .drafts/ is added to .gitignore automatically, so drafts never enter Git history.
  • Each user's draft is stored separately — multiple users can have independent drafts of the same file.
  • Drafts persist across browser sessions since they are stored server-side.

docs.title: File History
docs.text: Every document in Admont-AI is version-controlled with Git. You can view the full commit history of any file, compare changes between versions, and restore previous versions.

docs.title: Viewing History
docs.text:
1. Open a document.
  2. Click the menu (three dots) in the editor toolbar.
  3. Select Version history.

The history panel opens on the right, showing a chronological list of commits for the file. Each entry displays:

  • Commit message — What was changed
  • Author — Who made the change
  • Date — When the change was committed
  • Commit hash — The abbreviated Git commit identifier

docs.title: Comparing Versions
docs.text:
Click any commit in the history list to see a diff view. The editor switches to a side-by-side comparison showing:

  • The selected commit's version on the left
  • The current (HEAD) version on the right
  • Additions highlighted in green
  • Deletions highlighted in red

The diff header shows the commit details: message, author, and hash.

docs.title: Restoring a Version
docs.text:
To restore a previous version:

  1. Select the commit you want to restore in the history panel.
  2. Click the Restore button in the diff view header.

This copies the content from that commit into a new draft. You can review the restored content, make further edits if needed, and publish it as a new commit. The original history is preserved — restoring does not rewrite Git history.

docs.title: Closing the History View
docs.text: Click Close in the diff view header to return to the normal editor view.

docs.title: Technical Details
docs.text:
• History is fetched via GET /repos/:repo/filehistory/*path, which returns the Git log for that file.
  • Diffs are generated via GET /repos/:repo/filediff/*path?commit=<hash>, comparing the specified commit to HEAD.
  • File content at a specific commit is retrieved via GET /repos/:repo/fileatcommit/*path?commit=<hash>.
  • Restoring a version creates a draft with the old content — it does not force-push or rebase.

docs.title: Folders
docs.text: Folders organize documents within a repository. You can create, rename, move, and delete folders using the sidebar or the REST API.

docs.title: Creating a Folder
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the parent folder in the sidebar.
  2. Click the folder+ icon in the sidebar toolbar.
  3. Enter the folder name.

The folder is created immediately as a Git commit.

docs.title: Renaming a Folder
docs.text: Right-click (or use the context menu) on a folder in the sidebar and select Rename. Enter the new name. Renaming a folder updates all paths within it, including any permission entries in .file-permissions.yaml.

docs.title: Moving a Folder
docs.text: Drag the folder in the sidebar file tree and drop it into the target parent folder. The move is committed to Git, and permission entries are updated to reflect the new path.

docs.title: Deleting a Folder
docs.text: Right-click on a folder and select Delete. You'll be asked to confirm. Deleting a folder removes it and all its contents from the repository. Associated permission entries under that path are also removed.

docs.title: Drag-and-Drop
docs.text:
The sidebar file tree supports drag-and-drop for both files and folders:

  • Drag a file onto a folder to move it there.
  • Drag a folder onto another folder to move it (and all its contents).
  • During a drag, valid drop targets are highlighted.
  • The drop target must be a folder — you cannot drop onto a file.

docs.title: Sidebar Navigation
docs.text:
• Click a folder to expand or collapse it.
  • Click a file to open it in the editor.
  • The sidebar shows the full file tree for the currently selected repository.
  • Use the eye/eye-off toggle in the sidebar toolbar to show or hide non-content files (dotfiles, config files).

docs.title: Document Ordering
docs.text: Folders can contain an .order file that defines the display order of files and subfolders in the sidebar. Each line in the file is a filename or subfolder name. Items listed appear in that order; unlisted items appear alphabetically after the listed ones.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Markdown is a lightweight markup language created by John Gruber in 2004. It allows you to write formatted text using a plain-text editor with a simple, readable syntax that can be converted to HTML and many other formats. Markdown has become the de facto standard for writing documentation, README files, forum posts, and content across platforms like GitHub, GitLab, Reddit, and Stack Overflow.

The core philosophy behind Markdown is that the source text should be readable as-is, without needing to be rendered. Unlike HTML or LaTeX, Markdown files look clean and understandable even in their raw form.

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
• Easy to learn — The syntax is minimal and intuitive. Most people can pick it up in minutes.
  • Readable as plain text — Markdown files are human-readable even without rendering, making them ideal for version control.
  • Portable — Markdown is just plain text, so it works on any operating system and with any text editor.
  • Widely supported — GitHub, GitLab, Notion, Jira, Slack, and countless other tools support Markdown natively.
  • Convertible — Tools like Pandoc can convert Markdown to HTML, PDF, DOCX, LaTeX, and many other formats.
  • Version control friendly — Since Markdown is plain text, diffs are clean and meaningful in Git.
  • Focused writing — The simple syntax keeps you focused on content rather than formatting.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Limited styling — Markdown supports basic formatting but lacks fine-grained control over layout, colors, or typography.
  • No standard specification — Multiple flavors exist (CommonMark, GitHub Flavored Markdown, MultiMarkdown), which can lead to inconsistencies across platforms.
  • Complex tables are difficult — While basic tables are supported, complex layouts with merged cells or nested content are hard or impossible.
  • No native support for advanced features — Things like footnotes, math equations, or diagrams require extensions or specific flavors.
  • Embedded HTML as a workaround — When Markdown falls short, you often need to fall back to raw HTML, which defeats the purpose of simplicity.

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
Use # symbols to create headings. The number of # symbols determines the heading level.

# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4
##### Heading 5
###### Heading 6

docs.title: Text Formatting
docs.text:
**bold text**
*italic text*
***bold and italic***
~~strikethrough~~
`inline code`

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
[Link text](https://example.com)
[Link with title](https://example.com "Title text")

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
Unordered lists use -, *, or +:

- Item one
- Item two
  - Nested item
  - Another nested item
- Item three

Ordered lists use numbers:

1. First item
2. Second item
3. Third item

docs.title: Blockquotes
docs.text:
> This is a blockquote.
>
> It can span multiple lines.

docs.title: Code Blocks
docs.text:
Use triple backticks for fenced code blocks. Optionally specify the language for syntax highlighting:

```python
def hello():
    print("Hello, world!")
```

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Row 1    | Data     | Data     |
| Row 2    | Data     | Data     |

You can align columns using colons:

| Left     | Center   | Right    |
|:---------|:--------:|---------:|
| aligned  | aligned  | aligned  |

docs.title: Horizontal Rules
docs.text:
Use three or more dashes, asterisks, or underscores:

---
***
___

docs.title: Task Lists
docs.text:
- [x] Completed task
- [ ] Incomplete task
- [ ] Another task

docs.title: Images
docs.text:
Images use the same syntax as links, prefixed with !:

![Alt text](path/to/image.png)
![Alt text](path/to/image.jpg "Optional title")

You can reference images from a URL:

![Logo](https://example.com/logo.png)

Or use a relative path for images stored alongside your Markdown files:

![Diagram](./images/diagram.png)

To control image size, you need to fall back to HTML:

<img src="image.png" alt="Alt text" width="400">

docs.title: Videos
docs.text:
Markdown has no native video embedding syntax. There are several workarounds:

Link to the video with a thumbnail image:

[![Video title](https://img.youtube.com/vi/VIDEO_ID/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VIDEO_ID)

This displays a clickable thumbnail that opens the video.

Embed using HTML (where supported):

<video width="640" controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>

YouTube/Vimeo iframe (where HTML is allowed):

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/VIDEO_ID" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen></iframe>

Note: Not all Markdown renderers allow raw HTML. GitHub, for example, strips <iframe> and <video> tags for security reasons. In those cases, linking to the video is the most reliable approach.

docs.title: Audio
docs.text:
Similar to video, audio requires HTML:

<audio controls>
  <source src="audio.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
</audio>

docs.title: SVG and Other Formats
docs.text:
SVGs can be embedded as images:

![Diagram](diagram.svg)

Or inline using HTML for more control:

<img src="diagram.svg" alt="Diagram" width="600">

docs.title: Media and Uploads
docs.text: Admont-AI supports uploading and embedding images, videos, diagrams, and other binary files within your documents.

docs.title: Drag-and-Drop
docs.text:
Drag an image or file from your desktop directly into the markdown editor. The file is uploaded to the repository and an appropriate embed tag is inserted at the cursor position:

  • Images: ![filename](path/to/file.png)
  • Other files: a download link

docs.title: Upload Endpoint
docs.text: Files are uploaded via POST /repos/:repo/upload/*path with base64-encoded content. The backend commits the file to Git.

docs.title: Images
docs.text:
Images are displayed inline in the markdown editor. Supported formats:

  • PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, SVG

To embed an image, use standard markdown syntax:

![Alt text](./assets/screenshot.png)

Or use HTML for size control:

<img src="./assets/diagram.svg" alt="Diagram" width="600" />

docs.title: Image Editing
docs.text: When viewing an image file (not embedded in markdown), Admont-AI provides an annotation tool powered by TUI Image Editor. You can crop, rotate, add text, draw, and apply filters.

docs.title: Videos
docs.text:
Video files (.mp4, .webm, .mov) are played with the built-in HTML5 video player. To embed a video in a markdown document, use the HTML <video> tag:

<video width="640" controls>
  <source src="./assets/demo.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>

The markdown editor includes a video plugin that renders embedded videos inline.

docs.title: Mermaid
docs.text: Mermaid diagrams (.mmd files) are edited with a visual node editor (React Flow) alongside a code editor with live preview. You can create flowcharts, sequence diagrams, class diagrams, and more.

docs.title: Draw.io
docs.text: Draw.io diagrams (.drawio files) open in an embedded Draw.io editor for full visual editing. Changes are saved back to the repository as XML.

docs.title: SVG
docs.text: SVG files can be embedded as images in markdown or opened directly for viewing. When opened as a standalone file, SVGs are rendered in the image viewer.

docs.title: File Storage
docs.text: All uploaded files are committed to the Git repository alongside your markdown documents. There is no separate media storage — files follow the same version control, history, and permission rules as documents. Organize assets in an assets/ subfolder alongside related documents for clean structure.

docs.title: Search
docs.text: Admont-AI provides hybrid search across all indexed repositories, combining full-text keyword matching with AI-powered semantic search.

docs.title: Using Search
docs.text:
Click the search bar at the top of the page or use the keyboard shortcut to focus it. Type your query and results appear in real time (with a 300ms debounce).

Each result shows:

  • File path — The document and repository where the match was found
  • Excerpt — A text snippet showing the matching content
  • Relevance score — How closely the result matches your query

Click a result to navigate directly to that document.

docs.title: Search Modes
docs.text:
Mode How It Works Best For Fulltext PostgreSQL plainto_tsquery with ranking Exact keyword matches, specific terms Semantic Cosine similarity against query embedding vector Conceptual queries, "what is X", paraphrased content Hybrid (default) Runs both fulltext and semantic, merges with Reciprocal Rank Fusion General use — finds both exact and conceptual matches

Hybrid mode runs both search types at 2x the requested result limit, then merges them using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): each result's score is computed as Σ(1 / (60 + rank)). This balances exact keyword matches with semantically similar content.

docs.title: Scoping
docs.text: Search results span all repositories you have access to. Results from repositories where you lack read permission are automatically filtered out. Public repositories are searchable by all users, including anonymous ones.

docs.title: How Content Is Indexed
docs.text:
1. Chunking — Markdown files are parsed using the Goldmark AST parser and split into sections by heading hierarchy. Each chunk preserves its heading path (e.g., "Installation > Prerequisites"). Sections over 1000 characters are split at paragraph boundaries.

  2. Embedding — Each chunk is converted to a 384-dimensional vector using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ONNX model for semantic search.

  3. Storage — Chunks are stored in PostgreSQL with both a tsvector column (for full-text search) and a vector(384) column (for semantic search).

docs.title: When Indexing Happens
docs.text:
• On file change — Creating, updating, or deleting a file triggers incremental reindexing of that file's chunks.
  • On startup — The server detects files changed since the last indexed commit and reindexes them.
  • Manual reindex — Administrators can trigger a full reindex for any repository via the admin panel.

docs.title: Index Status
docs.text:
The search status endpoint reports the state of each repository's index:

Status Meaning Ready Index is up to date Rebuilding Full reindex in progress Updating Incremental update in progress Error Indexing failed (check server logs)

docs.title: Supported Search Engines
docs.text:
Engine Description pgvector PostgreSQL with local ONNX embeddings (default, no external dependencies) Elasticsearch 8.x External search cluster Typesense Lightweight external search engine

The search engine is configured per repository in the server configuration.
